chunk_summary: This section outlines the shared-care workflow for post-discharge nutrition monitoring of preterm/LBW/ELBW infants in India, emphasizing community worker tasks, clinician-led decisions, and standardized escalation thresholds for danger signs.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of community workers (ASHA/ANM/AWW) in monitoring post-discharge nutrition for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants.
Identify clinician-led tasks in feeding modality decisions and comorbidity management.
Learn standardized escalation thresholds for urgent evaluation of feeding-related danger signs.
Recognize the importance of aligning follow-up care with HBNC/HBYC guidelines.
key_facts: Community workers should record weight on MCP cards, confirm feed frequency, and screen for danger signs like fever, lethargy, and vomiting.
Clinician-led tasks include feeding modality decisions, fortification changes, and comorbidity management (e.g., BPD, CHD, NEC-related bowel disease).
Escalation thresholds include rapid weight loss, bilious vomiting, dehydration, fever with poor feeding, and suspected aspiration with cyanosis/apnea.
Post-discharge nutrition tiers (Tier 1/2/3) should be documented with measurable intake prescriptions (feeds/day, mL/feed, top-up triggers).
Community workers should not alter caloric density, tube plans, or medications but must escalate cases of weight plateau or feeding danger signs.
reference_quote: Community worker tasks (ASHA/ANM/AWW linked) should focus on observable items: weighing and recording on the MCP card, confirming feed frequency, and screening for danger signs (fever, lethargy, breathing difficulty, vomiting, diarrhea).
document_key_topics: Post-discharge nutrition monitoring for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants
Community worker roles in shared-care workflows
Standardized escalation thresholds for danger signs
